article.title: Cisco Agrees to Acquire Astrix Security, Adding a Non-Human Identity Layer to Its AI-Agent Defense Stack
article.summary: Cisco announced its intent to acquire Israeli startup Astrix Security on May 4 to extend zero trust to AI agents and machine identities. Terms were undisclosed; Calcalist reported approximately $400 million.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Cisco announced on May 4 that it has agreed to acquire Israeli cybersecurity startup Astrix Security, a five-year-old specialist in protecting the API keys, service accounts, and OAuth tokens that machine identities and AI agents use to log into enterprise systems. Cisco did not disclose financial terms; Calcalist reported the deal at approximately $400 million, [according to its coverage of the announcement](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581), while [SiliconANGLE noted](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) that The Information had earlier speculated the price could be "worth in the region of $300 million."

The deal slots Astrix's platform into Cisco's broader zero-trust security stack at a moment when enterprises are racing to deploy AI agents that hold credentials, call APIs, and execute work without a human in the loop — a category of identity that traditional access-management systems were not built to govern.

## What We Know

Astrix Security was founded in 2021 by Alon Jackson and Idan Gour, both veterans of the Israel Defense Forces' Unit 8200 signals-intelligence corps, [Calcalist reported](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581). The company raised more than $85 million across multiple rounds, including a $45 million Series B in late 2024 led by Menlo Ventures, [SecurityWeek wrote](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/), with additional investors that include Bessemer Venture Partners, CRV, and F2 Venture Capital, [per Calcalist](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581).

Astrix's platform is designed to inventory and govern what the security industry now calls non-human identities — software service accounts, API tokens, automated workflows, and AI agents — and to flag credentials that are over-privileged, dormant, or behaving suspiciously. The product covers "API keys, service accounts, and OAuth tokens" and provides "visibility into non-human identities and the activity of AI-driven agents, along with lifecycle management and automated detection and remediation of over-privileged, unnecessary, or malicious access," [SecurityWeek reported](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/).

Peter Bailey, Senior Vice President and General Manager of Cisco's Security Business Group, framed the rationale in agentic-AI terms. "The addition of Astrix Security brings deep capability to discover and secure every AI agent and non-human identity (NHI), including excessive privileges and real-time threats," Bailey said, [as quoted by SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/). The same statement also appeared in [Network World's coverage](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4166695/cisco-grabs-astrix-to-secure-ai-agents.html).

Cisco said it plans to integrate Astrix's technology into its Cisco Identity Intelligence platform and extend the capabilities into Cisco Secure Access and Duo Identity and Access Management, with telemetry feeding into Splunk or other security information and event management systems, [Network World reported](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4166695/cisco-grabs-astrix-to-secure-ai-agents.html). SiliconANGLE described the same integration roadmap, noting that Astrix's technology will "feed intelligence into Splunk or other SIEMs," [in its account of the announcement](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/).

The announcement leaned heavily on a statistical case for the urgency of non-human identity security. Cisco said "only 24% of organizations can control agent actions with proper guardrails" and "just 31% feel fully capable of securing their agent AI systems," while estimating that "agents and other NHIs outnumber humans 100:1" inside enterprises, [Network World reported](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4166695/cisco-grabs-astrix-to-secure-ai-agents.html).

## Why Non-Human Identity

The segment Astrix occupies has emerged in the past two years as enterprises wired together generative-AI tools, automation platforms, and SaaS integrations that talk to each other through machine credentials rather than human logins. Each AI agent that books a meeting, queries a database, or files an expense report does so under a service identity — and those identities, unlike human accounts, are rarely covered by single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, or routine access reviews.

Cisco described the gap as "extending zero trust principles to the emerging 'agentic workforce,' where AI agents and machine identities are rapidly expanding the enterprise attack surface," [SecurityWeek wrote](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/). Astrix's pitch, in turn, has been that the existing identity stack — built for humans authenticating with passwords or biometrics — cannot enforce least-privilege or detect anomalous behavior on credentials that may be created and discarded thousands of times an hour.

The company's product is structured around four pillars that Cisco intends to absorb wholesale: discovery and governance for AI agents; agentic access and lifecycle management; agentic threat detection and response; and centralized secrets management, [Network World reported](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4166695/cisco-grabs-astrix-to-secure-ai-agents.html).

## What We Don't Know

Cisco has not publicly disclosed the deal price. The two third-party figures circulating — "approximately $400 million" [from Calcalist](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581) and "in the region of $300 million" [via The Information as cited by SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) — differ by roughly a third, and neither has been confirmed by either company.

The expected close date for the transaction has not been announced. The official communication described the agreement as an "intent to acquire" rather than a completed transaction, [per Network World](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4166695/cisco-grabs-astrix-to-secure-ai-agents.html), which leaves regulatory and customary closing conditions outstanding. Headcount commitments, the future of Astrix's branding, and the geographic footprint of its Israeli engineering operations after integration have not been described in public statements.

## Analysis

The Astrix deal is the second time in roughly a year that Cisco has bolted an Israeli identity-and-detection company onto its security platform — and the first that explicitly targets the AI-agent layer rather than human users or endpoints. For Cisco, whose security business has grown around Duo, Secure Access, and the Splunk acquisition, Astrix offers a discrete capability that customers cannot easily build themselves: an inventory of every machine credential, scored for privilege and behavior, that can be surfaced inside the same tools that already govern human access.

For the Israeli cybersecurity ecosystem, the deal continues a long-running pattern of large U.S. acquirers picking up early-stage NHI and identity startups before they reach independent scale. Astrix joins Cisco at a price — whichever third-party figure proves correct — well above the more than $85 million it has raised across its private rounds, [per Calcalist](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581).

The broader question for buyers is whether the dedicated NHI-security category has converged with mainstream identity governance. Cisco's bet is that it has: by routing Astrix's discovery and policy enforcement through Cisco Identity Intelligence, the company is treating machine identities as a peer of human ones rather than a separate problem class. Whether competitors anchored in human identity, such as Okta or Microsoft, follow with comparable acquisitions will say a lot about how quickly the industry settles on a single architecture for governing the agentic workforce.
